one_line_takeaway: SafeMLLM is the first adversarial-training defense for MLLMs, using an embedding-level contrastive attack as the inner loop to fine-tune LoRA parameters, and reduces jailbreak ASR to near zero across six attacks and six models while keeping benign utility.
key_finding: SafeMLLM drives attack success rate to near zero across all six attacks and six models while largely preserving benign utility, substantially outperforming the safety-fine-tuning baseline VLGuard (which fails on white-box attacks) and LLM-only adversarial-training baselines R2D2 and CAT. On the four white-box attacks its all-6-model average ASR is 1.50% (ImgJP), 0.42% (VAA), 0.00% (GCG), and 0.17% (AutoDAN); on the two black-box image-text attacks it reaches 0.00% (FigStep) and 0.10% (MM-SafetyBench). Ablations show the contrastive loss is important for the weaker-aligned backbones (removing it raises average ASR to 13.67% from 0 on the ImgJP 13B setting), and removing the utility loss degrades benign GPT scores by up to 5.37 points.
core_contribution: The paper introduces SafeMLLM, which the authors position as the first adversarial-training framework for defending multimodal large language models (MLLMs) against jailbreak attacks in white-box settings. SafeMLLM alternates between two steps in a min-max style loop: (Step I) an inner attack step that generates worst-case adversarial perturbations via a newly proposed Contrastive Embedding Attack (CoE-Attack), and (Step II) a model-update step that fine-tunes LoRA parameters of the cross-modal adapter and LLM decoder to neutralize those perturbations while preserving benign utility. CoE-Attack is NOT presented as a standalone jailbreak attack contribution; it is the inner-maximization component of the defense loop. Its novelty over prior adversarial-training-for-LLMs work is (a) injecting adversarial noise directly at the token-embedding level via two matrices (Ph before the query, acting as an adversarial image; Pt after the query, acting as an adversarial suffix), avoiding costly greedy vocabulary search and vision-encoder passes, and (b) a contrastive loss that relatively suppresses refusal responses without collapsing the model into nonsense output.
threat_model: White-box jailbreak attacks on MLLMs. The defender fine-tunes a model and then publicly releases the tuned parameters and gradient information; the adversary is assumed to have full access to model weights and gradients and crafts perturbations on the image modality, text modality, or both to bypass safety guardrails and elicit harmful/illegal content. The paper argues prior defenses (content filtering, safety fine-tuning such as VLGuard, and RLHF) fail against white-box perturbations, motivating adversarial tuning. Explicitly out of scope: audio and video modalities, and non-jailbreak security threats.
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: SafeMLLM only handles image- and text-based attacks and may be ineffective against audio/video modalities; and it targets only jailbreak defense, not broader MLLM security threats. Reviewer-observed caveats: robustness gains are largest on weaker backbones, since on LLaVA (built on safety-aligned Vicuna-1.5) removing modules barely changes ASR, indicating some of the benefit overlaps with pre-existing alignment; ASR toxicity labels and utility scores both depend on gpt-4-turbo as judge; evaluation uses only the first 40-100 prompts of each dataset; VLGuard is only comparable on the two released LLaVA models (hence two average columns), so the all-6 average excludes VLGuard.

datasets_used: AdvBench (first 100 prompts; used for ImgJP, GCG, AutoDAN)
Harmful Instructions (40 toxic prompts; used for VAA)
SafeBench-Tiny (50 image-text harmful queries; used for FigStep)
quantitative_results: SafeMLLM ImgJP all-6-model avg ASR = 1.50% (vs Original 64.00%, R2D2 32.00%, CAT 19.83%); reported avg improvement 17.6%
SafeMLLM VAA all-6-model avg ASR = 0.42% (vs Original 35.83%, R2D2 12.00%, CAT 4.58%); reported avg improvement 4.2%
SafeMLLM GCG all-6-model avg ASR = 0.00% (vs Original 59.00%, R2D2 23.17%, CAT 9.67%); reported avg improvement 9.7%
SafeMLLM AutoDAN all-6-model avg ASR = 0.17% (vs Original 81.17%, R2D2 41.33%, CAT 26.00%); reported avg improvement 25.8%
SafeMLLM FigStep (black-box) all-6-model avg ASR = 0.00% (vs Original 35.00%, R2D2 26.00%, CAT 18.67%)
SafeMLLM MM-SafetyBench (black-box) all-6-model avg ASR = 0.10% (vs Original 16.46%, R2D2 11.42%, CAT 7.92%)
VLGuard fails on white-box attacks on LLaVA-7B: ImgJP ASR 88.00%, GCG ASR 79.00%, AutoDAN ASR 81.00% (but strong on black-box FigStep 2.00% and MM-SafetyBench 0.00%)
Ablation (ImgJP, 13B): removing contrastive loss raises average ASR to 13.67% (rows 23.00 MiniGPT-v4 / 18.00 InstructBLIP / 0.00 LLaVA) vs 0.00 for full SafeMLLM
Utility ablation (13B): removing Lutility drops GPT utility scores to 2.10/1.97/7.29 (MiniGPT-v4/InstructBLIP/LLaVA) vs full 6.81/7.34/7.45, largest gap 5.37 points
SafeMLLM shows lower ASR on 13B than 7B models, attributed to more trainable parameters aiding adversarial training
baselines_compared: Original MLLM (no adversarial training / no defense)
VLGuard (Zong et al., 2024) - safety fine-tuning on toxic-image/safe-response dataset; only released for LLaVA-7B/13B
R2D2 (Mazeika et al., 2024) - LLM-decoder adversarial training then reattached to visual encoder
CAT (Xhonneux et al., 2024) - continuous-attack LLM adversarial training then reattached
